channel.title: Science
channel.description: The very best in news and research across the sciences.

item.title: How much killing will it take to save the spotted owl?
item.description: In a massive experiment, biologists are shooting thousands of aggressive barred owls—hoping to create a spotted owl “safe zone”

item.title: Adaptation across an extreme elevational gradient in Andean leaf-eared mice, the world’s highest-dwelling mammal | Science
item.description: Andean leaf-eared mice (Phyllotis vaccarum) live at the highest elevations of any mammal, and they also have the broadest elevational range, from sea level to mountain summits of >6700 meters. Highland populations have evolved an enhanced thermogenic ...

item.title: A statistical test for the benefits of personalizing interventions | Science
item.description: From medicine to marketing to social sciences, the promise of tailoring interventions to individuals is undeniable. However, practical applications force weighing personalization’s potential benefits with its possible increased cost and fragility. We ...

item.title: The CARM1 epigenetic enzyme inhibits cross-presenting dendritic cell function in cancer immunity | Science
item.description: The cancer-immunity cycle requires cross-presenting type I conventional dendritic cells (cDC1s) that induce T cell–mediated immunity, but therapeutic strategies for enhancing intratumoral cDC1 function are currently inadequate. We found the epigenetic ...

item.title: Neural circuits for valence updating in social memory | Science
item.description: Social animals recognize familiar conspecifics and selectively avoid harmful ones. As social relationships shift, continuous updating of social valence is essential, yet the underlying neural mechanisms remain unclear. Here, by artificially transforming ...

item.title: Roots navigate around decay regions by sensing local pH gradients | Science
item.description: Plant tropisms enable roots to navigate complex soils by responding to directional environmental cues. Biological decay, although central to nutrient cycling, also creates microbially active and potentially hostile niches. In this work, we identified “...

item.title: C1q and immunoglobulins mediate activity-dependent synapse loss in the adult brain | Science
item.description: Complement component 1q (C1q), the initiator of the classical complement cascade, mediates synaptic elimination in development and disease, yet the triggers for its deposition on synapses remain unclear. Using in vivo chemogenetics, we demonstrate that ...

item.title: Prison release is an opportunity for addiction research | Science
item.description: Imprisonment and subsequent release from prison are powerful interventions. They are the social equivalent of major surgery. For prisoners with pre-existing drug addiction problems, time in prison can break a pattern of regular substance use and enable them to rebuild constructive family and social connections, although it often does not equip them with the skills and resources to avoid addiction problems after release. However, incarceration can also connect this vulnerable population with more-criminal networks that will continue to feed them following release. As with surgery, both the nature and the quality of the process matter. For example, people released from US prisons that do not offer within-prison medication treatment for opioid use disorders are twice as likely to die following their release compared with those who received medical support, with the greater risk in the first month post-release. The prison experience and the return from prison to the broader community are extraordinary research opportunities.

item.title: Transient assembly of precision-tuned platinum-skin intermetallic catalysts for fuel cells | Science
item.description: Highly efficient catalysts require precisely engineered intricate structures, yet conventional thermodynamically controlled syntheses often involve cumbersome procedures and limited structural precision. We report a nonequilibrium transient assembly ...

item.title: A phase microscope for quantum gases | Science
item.description: Coherence properties are central to quantum systems and are at the heart of phenomena such as superconductivity. In this work, we studied coherence properties of an ultracold Bose gas in a two-dimensional optical lattice across the thermal phase ...

item.title: In Other Journals | Science
item.description: Editors’ selections from the current scientific literature

item.title: In Science Journals | Science
item.description: Highlights from the Science family of journals

item.title: EU horse legislation compromises ecosystem restoration efforts | Science
item.description: EU legislation permits and supports the extensive use of domestic horses (Equus ferus caballus) for sport, recreation, and leisure but constrains their existence as free-ranging or rewilded populations (1). This asymmetry reflects a conceptualization of horses as managed property rather than ecological agents. As a result, legislation enables the intensive personal use of horses while limiting their potential participation in ecological restoration opportunities and alternative modes of existence. This contradiction is increasingly salient under the European Nature Restoration Law (2), which calls for large-scale ecosystem recovery but does not accommodate the functional role of horses in ecosystems, where they historically filled an important niche as large herbivore grazers (3).

item.title: US military expansion and deep-sea mining pose environmental risks to Guam | Science
item.description: The US military has contributed to ecosystem destruction, biodiversity loss, and population-level declines of many species (1–3). Its activities have polluted drinking water, contaminated local fisheries (4–6), and negatively affected the health and well-being of many communities worldwide (3, 5–7). One estimate suggests that the pollution produced by the US military-industrial complex is equivalent to that of approximately 140 countries (8).

item.title: Prioritizing a vital river’s restoration | Science
item.description: HomeScienceVol. 393, No. 6807Prioritizing a vital river’s restorationBack To Vol. 393, No. 6807 Full accessLetter Share on Prioritizing a vital river’s restorationZhi-Tao Wang and Ping ZhuangAuthors Info & AffiliationsScience9 Jul 2026Vol 393, Issue 6807pp. 153-154DOI: 10.1126/science.aei3599 PREVIOUS ARTICLEDoes AI work for us, or do we work for AI?PreviousNEXT ARTICLEUS military expansion and d…

item.title: Does AI work for us, or do we work for AI? | Science
item.description: There is little enthusiasm for a technology that turns human users into “reverse centaurs,” argues a critic

item.title: What do we know about sex? | Science
item.description: It depends on whom you ask

item.title: Bridging data gaps to support the Global Plastics Treaty | Science
item.description: Reporting should consider data harmonization, supply chain metrics, and pollution hotspot identification

item.title: The hidden logic of emotion | Science
item.description: Internal emotion models unify research across species and spark fresh ideas for treating anxiety and trauma

item.title: Antibodies sculpt adult brain circuits | Science
item.description: Immune cells target hyperactive neurons to eliminate synaptic connections

item.title: Ice as a geochemical reactor | Science
item.description: Freezing redirects the fate of iron in Earth’s cryosphere

item.title: To eat or to breathe? | Science
item.description: In highland mice, the responses to low oxygen and toxins compete for a shared molecular regulator

item.title: Why cities cannot learn | Science
item.description: For most of the world’s urban history, changes in policies and responses to hazards have proceeded on a timescale that made adaptation possible, however slow or politically contested. The Great Fire of London in 1666 produced building regulations within ...

item.title: As flu virus threatens its unique bird species, New Zealand pins hopes on a vaccine
item.description: Arrival of H5N1 in Australia triggers ambitious vaccination program

item.title: Uranus and Neptune may not be ‘ice giants’ after all
item.description: A growing number of theories propose Uranus and Neptune are rocky worlds

item.title: British ‘First Fleet’ brought smallpox to Australia—and may have killed millions
item.description: Two papers pin the deadly disease’s introduction on British colonists and suggest the continent held far more people than previously believed

item.title: Paleontologist Neil Shubin vows to keep National Academy of Sciences relevant
item.description: New NAS president discusses the future of the beleaguered institution and the precarious state of U.S. science under Trump

item.title: USDA accelerates plan to close its flagship scientific campus
item.description: Agency says closing the Beltsville Agricultural Research Center will improve efficiency, but skeptics argue it will undercut research critical to farmers

item.title: The origin, history, and resistance architecture of an invasive urban malaria mosquito in Africa | Science
item.description: The invasive urban malaria vector Anopheles stephensi threatens 126 million city dwellers in Africa. Controlling An. stephensi requires greater understanding of its origin, invasion dynamics, and insecticide resistance mechanisms. Analysis of 645 whole ...

item.title: Long-range extended chains arising from polymerization-driven spontaneous assembly | Science
item.description: A central challenge for conjugated polymers is to achieve long-range order while remaining solution processable, which is essential for matching the electrical performance of their counterparts of crystalline inorganic semiconductors. In this work, we ...
